meta.title: F.4 Containers, VMs, and Sandboxes
meta.description: Map the isolation spectrum from sandboxed iframes to Firecracker microVMs, and understand why the choice between containers, V8 isolates, and WebContainers determines Make's cost structure, startup latency, and security posture for executing untrusted AI-generated code.

blocks.prompt:
> You need to run untrusted, AI-generated code from 100,000 concurrent users. Each user's code must be completely isolated from every other user's. What are your options?

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module maps the isolation spectrum — from sandboxed iframes to Firecracker microVMs — and explains why the choice between isolation models is the foundational architectural decision of the preview platform. By the end, you'll be able to evaluate trade-offs between security, startup latency, and cost, and explain why Make likely needs a hybrid architecture rather than a single isolation model.

The isolation model is the infrastructure bet that determines cost structure, security posture, and language support simultaneously — choosing between VMs, containers, microVMs, and browser sandboxes is not an engineering implementation detail but the foundational architectural decision of the preview platform. No one has solved it perfectly. The differences between v0, Bolt, Replit, and Make's preview environments trace back to how each team answered this question.

**The Threat Model**

AI-generated code is untrusted by definition. The AI occasionally produces code that:
- Makes outbound HTTP requests (data exfiltration risk)
- Reads environment variables (credential exposure risk)
- Spawns child processes (privilege escalation risk)
- Consumes unbounded CPU or memory (resource exhaustion, denial of service)
- Accesses the filesystem arbitrarily

In a multi-tenant environment where user A's code runs on the same infrastructure as user B's code, any of these failures could affect other users or the platform itself. Isolation is not a nice-to-have — it is the security primitive that makes the product viable.

**Virtual Machines: Maximum Isolation, Maximum Cost**

A virtual machine (VM — a software emulation of a complete computer, running its own operating system on virtualized hardware provided by a hypervisor) runs a complete operating system kernel on virtualized hardware. The hypervisor (KVM, VMware, Hyper-V — software that abstracts physical hardware and presents each VM with its own virtual CPU, memory, disk, and network) abstracts the physical hardware. Two VMs on the same physical host are completely isolated at the kernel level — a compromise of one VM does not give access to the other.

Tradeoffs:
- **Startup time:** 30–90 seconds. Booting an OS is not fast.
- **Memory:** A minimal Ubuntu VM uses 256MB of RAM before your application starts.
- **Cost:** Running a VM per user preview is economically impossible at scale. A platform with 100K concurrent previews would require an enormous fleet.
- **Isolation strength:** Excellent. This is the gold standard.

VMs are appropriate for long-running workspaces (Replit's persistent development environments) where startup cost is amortized over hours of use. They are not appropriate for ephemeral previews.

**Containers: The Practical Standard**

Docker containers (lightweight, portable execution environments that share the host OS kernel but isolate processes, filesystems, and network using Linux kernel features called namespaces and cgroups) share the host OS kernel. Instead of virtualizing hardware, containers use kernel namespaces (which hide processes and filesystems from each other) and cgroups (which enforce CPU and memory limits) to isolate process trees, filesystems, network interfaces, and resource limits.

Container startup time: 1–5 seconds for a warm image (already pulled to the host). Cold starts (first pull) can take 10–30 seconds.

Containers are the current standard for cloud workloads because the density is high — a single host can run hundreds of containers — and the startup time is acceptable for most use cases. Kubernetes (the dominant container orchestration system — schedules containers across clusters of machines, handles scaling, health checks, and networking) orchestrates containers at scale.

The security limitation: containers share the kernel. A kernel exploit on the host affects all containers. This is not a theoretical risk — container escapes are a real attack vector. For most enterprise workloads, containers are secure enough. For executing arbitrary untrusted code at scale, they may not be.

blocks.question: Containers share the host OS kernel while VMs run separate kernels. Why does this distinction matter for Make's threat model — and in what scenario would the difference between them become an actual incident?
blocks.answer: In Make's threat model, the concern is that user A's generated code could escape its container and access user B's data or the host system. With containers, if an attacker finds a kernel vulnerability (a 'container escape'), they can gain access to the host — and from there, potentially to every container running on it. With VMs, each workload runs its own kernel instance; a kernel exploit in one VM doesn't reach others. The incident scenario: a sophisticated attacker generates code specifically designed to exploit a known kernel vulnerability, runs it in their Make preview, gains host access, and reads other users' environment variables or session data. This is why platforms running truly untrusted code at scale (like AWS Lambda) use VM-level isolation (Firecracker), not containers.

blocks.content:
**Microcontainers: Firecracker and the MicroVM Model**

Firecracker is an open-source Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) developed by AWS and used in Lambda and Fargate. It creates "microVMs" — lightweight VMs that use the KVM hypervisor but without the overhead of a traditional OS. A Firecracker microVM can start in under 150 milliseconds and uses as little as 5MB of memory overhead.

Firecracker gives VM-level isolation (separate kernel per microVM) at near-container startup speed. This is what makes AWS Lambda's security model viable — each Lambda invocation runs in its own Firecracker microVM.

For Make's preview infrastructure, Firecracker is the most compelling model: per-user isolation at VM-level security with sub-second startup. The operational complexity is real — you're running a VMM, not Docker — but the security-to-performance tradeoff is better than any alternative for untrusted code execution at scale.

**Browser Sandboxes: WebContainers and Iframes**

StackBlitz's WebContainers technology takes a radically different approach: run the entire Node.js environment inside the browser tab using WebAssembly (WASM — a binary format that runs at near-native speed in browsers, enabling languages like C++ and Rust to execute in web environments). The user's code never leaves their browser. There are no server compute costs for execution. Isolation is provided by the browser's security model.

A browser tab is already a highly isolated sandbox:
- No direct filesystem access (sandboxed filesystem API only)
- Network requests subject to CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a browser security policy that restricts which domains a web page can make requests to)
- No process spawning
- Memory and CPU limited by the browser's process model

WebContainers implement a custom OS-like environment in WebAssembly: a virtual filesystem, Node.js APIs (reimplemented in Wasm), and a package resolution system. npm packages that require native bindings (compiled C/C++ addons, sometimes called `.node` files) cannot run. Packages that rely on Node.js internals may behave differently.

Iframe sandboxes are a cruder mechanism: an `<iframe sandbox="allow-scripts">` attribute restricts what code in the iframe can do — no cookies, no localStorage, no top-frame navigation. Make can run preview code in a sandboxed iframe within the Figma canvas for basic isolation, but this only prevents cross-origin interaction — it does not prevent resource exhaustion or malicious network requests.

## How to Think About This

Every isolation model trades one of three things: security, speed, or cost. Full VMs are the most secure and the most expensive. Browser sandboxes are the cheapest and have the weakest isolation. Firecracker is the current best answer for platforms that need VM-level security at near-container speed — it achieves this by stripping the traditional VM's OS overhead rather than sharing the host kernel. The PM's job is to match the isolation model to the threat model: what is the actual worst-case exploit, and how much are we willing to pay per user session to prevent it?

**V8 Isolates and Cloudflare Workers**

V8 Isolates are the lightest-weight isolation unit. Each isolate (a V8 execution context — isolated memory heap, isolated global object, but sharing the same V8 engine process) starts in under 1 millisecond and uses minimal memory.

Cloudflare Workers runs on V8 Isolates. Every Workers function is a separate isolate; thousands of them can run on a single machine. This is how Cloudflare achieves sub-millisecond cold starts and extreme density.

The constraint: V8 Isolates are JavaScript/WebAssembly only. No Node.js APIs. No arbitrary binaries. No filesystem. The execution environment is extremely restricted. For running npm packages that use Node.js APIs, Workers cannot help you.

**The Tradeoff Chart**

| Technology | Isolation | Startup | Cost/1K users | Language Support |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full VM | ★★★★★ | 30–90s | Very High | Any |
| Container (Docker) | ★★★☆☆ | 1–5s | Medium | Any |
| Firecracker microVM | ★★★★★ | 100–500ms | Medium | Any |
| WebContainers | ★★★☆☆ | < 1s | Near zero (client) | JS/TS only |
| V8 Isolates | ★★☆☆☆ | < 5ms | Very Low | JS/TS/Wasm only |
| Sandboxed iframe | ★☆☆☆☆ | Instant | Zero | Any (browser) |

Make's preview architecture likely uses a hybrid: sandboxed iframes for initial display, with server-side execution (Firecracker or containers) for code that requires Node.js APIs or server components. The platform PM's job is to know where the boundaries are and when to invest in moving up the isolation stack.

---

blocks.question: Make's team is debating whether to use V8 Isolates (like Cloudflare Workers) for preview execution instead of containers. An engineer says "Isolates are 100× cheaper and start instantly." A security engineer says "but we can't run npm packages with native addons." As PM, how do you frame the decision?
blocks.answer:
This is a capability vs. cost tradeoff that maps to user segments. V8 Isolates work for the majority of generated code (React, Tailwind, fetch-based data loading) but fail for code with native dependencies (bcrypt, sharp, sqlite3). The PM question is: what percentage of Make's generated apps require native Node.js packages? If the AI agent defaults to pure-JS alternatives (argon2 in Wasm instead of bcrypt native), the isolate model works for most users. You'd architect a tiered system: isolates for standard previews, containers as fallback for complex apps. Cost and security both favor isolates as the default.

---

blocks.title:
Open StackBlitz and create a new project. Open DevTools → Network tab. Notice that node_modules content is being served from the browser's local cache — nothing is going to a server for package resolution. Now try importing a package with native bindings (like `bcrypt`). Observe what fails and why.

---
blocks.solution: When you open StackBlitz and create a new project, check the Network tab — you'll see package files loading from `/_node_modules/` URLs that resolve to the browser's cache or a service worker (a script that runs in the background and intercepts network requests), not from a remote npm registry. This is WebContainers in action: the entire Node.js resolution mechanism is running inside your browser tab. When you add `bcrypt` to your `package.json` and try to import it, you'll see one of two failure patterns: either an install error in the terminal ("Could not install bcrypt — native addons not supported") or a runtime error when you try to use it ("Error: Cannot find module './bcrypt_lib.node'"). The `.node` file extension is the tell — it's a compiled C++ binary, and WebAssembly cannot execute arbitrary native binaries compiled for a specific CPU architecture. This is the hard boundary of the WebContainers model. Notice that pure JavaScript packages like `lodash`, `date-fns`, or `zod` install and run perfectly — the limitation is specific to packages with compiled native bindings, which are relatively rare but show up in security-sensitive contexts (password hashing), image processing, and database drivers.

blocks.prompt:
Explain to a Figma design partner why "running your code in the browser" (WebContainers) is a fundamentally different security model than "running your code on our servers" — and what the practical implications are for what kinds of apps Make can preview.

---

questions: Think of a product or service you've built or worked on that had to run untrusted third-party code or content — whether from users, integrations, or AI-generated output. How did your team approach the isolation problem, and what tradeoff did you make between security, cost, and startup latency?

The three dimensions of this problem are security, performance, and cost — and each isolation model sacrifices at least one. Which of the three does Make most have to sacrifice given its 'instant preview' product promise, and how would you communicate that tradeoff to a skeptical security team?

---

blocks.content:
The isolation model is the infrastructure bet that determines cost structure, security posture, and language support simultaneously — choosing between VMs, containers, microVMs, and browser sandboxes is not an engineering implementation detail but the foundational architectural decision of the preview platform.

---

blocks.prompt:
> Code runs. Preview works. Now the user clicks "Deploy." That URL has to resolve globally in under a second, serve the right content, and handle a user in Tokyo as gracefully as one in San Francisco. That's the CDN and edge network problem.

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: What is the primary purpose of isolation in Make's preview infrastructure?
options: To ensure each user's preview loads faster by dedicating CPU resources to a single process
To prevent untrusted AI-generated code from one user's session from accessing data, processes, or resources belonging to other users or the host system
To compress files before sending them to the browser so previews load faster over slow connections
reviewQuestions.explanation: AI-generated code is untrusted by definition — it may make outbound HTTP requests, read environment variables, spawn child processes, or consume unbounded resources. In a multi-tenant environment where thousands of users run code on shared infrastructure, isolation is the security primitive that prevents one user's code from affecting others. Without it, a single malicious prompt could compromise the entire platform.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — isolation isn't a performance feature, it's the security primitive that makes running untrusted code at scale viable at all.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Isolation is a security concern, not a performance or compression mechanism. Its purpose is to ensure that user A's generated code — which may be malicious or simply buggy — cannot reach user B's data, credentials, or processes. That multi-tenant security boundary is what makes the product safe to operate.

reviewQuestions.question: Approximately how fast can a Firecracker microVM start, and what makes this possible?
options: 30–90 seconds, because each microVM boots a full Ubuntu operating system from scratch
1–5 seconds, because Firecracker containers share the host OS kernel the same way Docker does
Under 150 milliseconds, because Firecracker creates minimal VMs using KVM without the overhead of a full OS device model
reviewQuestions.explanation: Firecracker achieves sub-150ms startup by stripping the traditional VM's overhead — no emulated BIOS, no USB, minimal virtual devices — while still running each microVM on its own kernel via KVM. This gives VM-level isolation (separate kernel per workload) at near-container speed. It's the model AWS uses for Lambda, and it's the most compelling architecture for platforms that need both security and fast cold starts.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Good — Firecracker's minimal device model is the engineering insight that closes the gap between VM isolation and container speed.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Firecracker is much faster than a full VM boot and uses a different mechanism than Docker. It runs each microVM on its own kernel (providing VM-level isolation) but strips away the full OS initialization overhead, achieving startup times under 150ms. Docker shares the host kernel; Firecracker does not — that's the key distinction.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes a key difference between containers (Docker) and full virtual machines for Make's threat model?
options: Containers are more secure than VMs because they use read-only filesystems that prevent code from making changes
Containers share the host OS kernel, so a kernel exploit could allow a container escape affecting all containers on that host; VMs run separate kernel instances, so a compromise stays within that VM
VMs are weaker than containers because a VM can be paused and resumed, making it vulnerable to state inspection attacks
reviewQuestions.explanation: The critical security difference is kernel sharing. Docker containers use Linux namespaces and cgroups to isolate processes, but they all share the same host kernel. A kernel vulnerability exploited inside a container can potentially grant access to the host — and from there, to every container on it. VMs run their own kernel instance; a kernel exploit in one VM does not reach others. For platforms running truly untrusted code, this distinction determines the realistic threat surface.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — the shared kernel is the architectural reason container security has limits for untrusted code execution, and it's why Firecracker-style microVMs are the preferred model for platforms like Lambda.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The key difference is kernel sharing. Containers share the host OS kernel — which means a kernel exploit inside a container can potentially reach the host and other containers. VMs run fully separate kernel instances, providing a stronger isolation boundary. Container filesystems aren't inherently read-only, and VM pause/resume isn't a primary threat vector in this context.
